title: Welcome to Docker
description: Welcome to the Docker Simulator! In this first level, you'll learn how to start a container. Docker containers are isolated environments that run applications.
objective: Start a container using the command: `docker run ubuntu`

title: Containerizing an Application
description: Let's package a real application! Imagine we have code for a Node.js Todo App in our current directory. We need to build a Docker image for it so we can run it anywhere.
objective: Build a Docker image named `todo-app` by running: `docker build -t todo-app .`

title: Running the Application
description: Now that our Todo App is packaged into a Docker image, let's run it! We'll use the `-d` flag to run it in the background (detached), the `-p` flag to map port 3000 to our host machine, and `--name` to name it 'my-todo'.
objective: Run the app: `docker run -d -p 3000:3000 --name my-todo todo-app`

title: Updating the Application
description: You found a bug in the code! To deploy a fix, you must first stop and remove the old running container.
objective:
1. Run `docker stop my-todo`
2. Run `docker rm my-todo`

title: Sharing the Application
description: Your Todo App is fixed and ready for production! Let's upload it to Docker Hub so your teammates can pull it and use it.
objective: Push the image to the registry: `docker push todo-app`

title: Persisting the Database
description: When a container is removed, all its data is deleted. To permanently save your Todo list items, we must mount a Docker Volume inside the container.
objective:
1. Run `docker volume create todo-db`
2. Run `docker run -v todo-db:/etc/todos ubuntu`

title: Multi-Container Apps (Networking)
description: Our app is growing. We want to separate the database from the web app. To let containers talk to each other securely, we create custom networks.
objective:
1. Run `docker network create todo-net`
2. Run `docker run --network todo-net todo-app`

title: Docker Compose
description: Typing `docker run`, `network create`, and `volume create` manually is exhausting. Docker Compose orchestrates your entire architecture in one command using a YAML file.
objective: Start the entire multi-container architecture at once: `docker compose up`
